2.1: As the time of his death drew near, David gave his instructions to Solomon, his son, saying:
2.2: «"I am going the way of all the earth, show yourself strong and be a man.".
2.3: Keep the service of the Lord your God, walking in his ways, observing his statutes, his commandments, his ordinances, and his precepts, according to what is written in the Law of Moses, so that you may prosper in all that you do and wherever you turn,
2.4: so that the Lord may fulfill his word which he spoke concerning me, saying: If your sons take heed to their way, walking before me faithfully, with all their heart and with all their soul, you will never lack a descendant to sit on the throne of Israel.
2.5: You yourself know what Joab son of Zervia did to me, what he did to the two commanders of the armies of Israel, to Abner son of Ner and to Amasa son of Jether: he killed them, shedding the blood of war in peacetime and putting the blood of war on the belt that was around his waist and on the sandals that were on his feet.
2.6: You will act according to your wisdom and you will not let her white hair descend in peace to the realm of the dead.
2.7: But you shall show kindness to the sons of Berzelai the Gileadite, and they shall be among those who eat at your table, for that is how they came to me when I was fleeing from Absalom your brother.
2.8: Behold, you have with you Shemei, son of Gera, a Benjamite from Bahurim. He uttered fierce curses against me on the day I went to Mahanaim. But when he came down to meet me at the Jordan, I swore to him by the Lord, saying, I will not put you to death with the sword.
2.9: And now you will not let him go unpunished, for you are a wise man and you know how you ought to deal with him; in blood you will bring down his gray hair to the realm of the dead.»
2.10: David rested with his ancestors and was buried in the city of David.
2.11: The time that David reigned over Israel was forty years: he reigned seven years in Hebron and he reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem.
2.12: And Solomon sat on the throne of David, his father, and his kingdom was firmly established.
2.13: Adonijah, son of Haggith, came to Bathsheba, Solomon's mother. She said to him, "Have you come with peaceful intentions?" He replied, "Yes, with peaceful intentions."«
2.14: And he added, "I have something to say to you." She said, "Speak."«
2.15: And he said, «You know that the kingdom belonged to me and that all Israel looked to me to reign. But the kingdom has been transferred and given to my brother, because the Lord had destined it for him.”.
2.16: "Now I ask only one thing of you: don't reject me." She replied, "Speak."»
2.17: And he said, «Please tell King Solomon, for he will not reject you, to give me Abishag the Shunammite as my wife.»
2.18: Bathsheba said, "Very well. I will speak to the king about you."«
2.19: Bathsheba went to King Solomon to speak to him about Adonijah. And the king rose to meet her and bowed down before her, sat on his throne, and made a throne for the king's mother, and she sat at his right hand.
2.20: Then she said, "I have a small request to make of you: do not reject me." The king said to her, "Ask, my mother, for I will not reject you."«
2.21: She said, "Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to Adonijah, your brother, as his wife."«
2.22: King Solomon answered and said to his mother, «Why do you ask for Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? Ask for the kingship for him, for he is my older brother, for him, for Abiathar the priest, and for Joab son of Zeruiah.»
2.23: King Solomon swore by the Lord, saying, «May God deal with me in all his severity if Adonijah has not spoken this word to his own destruction.
2.24: »And now, as surely as the Lord lives, who has established me and placed me on the throne of my father David and built me a house as he promised, this very day Adonijah shall be put to death.”
2.25: And King Solomon sent Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, to strike down Adonijah, who died.
2.26: The king said to the priest Abiathar, «Go back to Anathoth to your own land, for you deserve to die, but I will not put you to death today, because you carried the ark of the Lord God before David my father and because you shared in all that my father suffered.»
2.27: And Solomon drove out Abiathar from being a priest of the Lord, thus fulfilling the word that the Lord had spoken concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh.
2.28: The news reached Joab, for Joab had sided with Adonijah, though he had not sided with Absalom. And Joab fled to the tabernacle of the Lord and seized the horns of the altar.
2.29: King Solomon was told that Joab had taken refuge in the tabernacle of the Lord and was near the altar, and Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada, saying, «Go, strike him down.»
2.30: When Benaiah arrived at the tabernacle of the Lord, he said to Joab, «This is what the king says: Come out.» But he replied, «No, I will die here.» Benaiah reported this answer to the king, saying, «This is what Joab said, this is what he answered me.»
2.31: And the king said to Banaiah, «Do as he said, strike him and bury him, and you will thus remove from me and from my father’s house the blood that Joab shed without cause.
2.32: The Lord will bring his blood back on his own head, he who struck down two men more righteous and better than himself and killed them with the sword, without my father David knowing it: Abner, son of Ner, commander of the army of Israel, and Amasa, son of Jether, commander of the army of Judah.
2.33: Their blood will fall on the head of Joab and on the head of his descendants forever, but there will be peace forever from the Lord for David and his descendants, for his house and his throne.»
2.34: Banaiah, son of Jehoiada, went up and struck Joab, killing him, and he was buried in his house in the desert.
2.35: And the king appointed Benaiah son of Jehoiada in his place as commander of the army, and the king appointed Zadok the priest in place of Abiathar.
2.36: The king sent for Shemei and said to him, «Build yourself a house in Jerusalem, and you shall dwell there and not leave it to go here and there.
2.37: »The day you go out and cross the Kidron Valley, know that you will certainly die; your blood will be on your own head.”
2.38: Shemei answered the king, "That is a good word; what my lord the king says, your servant will do." And Shemei remained in Jerusalem for many days.
2.39: After three years, two of Shemei's servants fled to Achish, son of Maah, king of Geth. This was reported to Shemei, with the words, "Your servants are in Geth."«
2.40: Shemei got up and, having saddled his donkey, went to Geth, to Achish, to look for his servants. Shemei went and brought back his servants from Geth.
2.41: Solomon was told that Shemei had gone from Jerusalem to Geth and had returned.
2.42: The king sent for Shemei and said to him, «Did I not make you swear by the Lord and did I not give you a command, saying, ‘On the day you go out to go to one side or the other, know that you will certainly die’? And did you not answer me, ‘The word that I heard is good’?”
2.43: Why then did you not keep the oath you swore to the Lord and the command I gave you?»
2.44: And the king said to Shemei, «You know, your heart knows, all the evil that you did to David, my father, the Lord will bring back your wickedness on your own head.
2.45: But King Solomon will be blessed, and the throne of David will be established forever before the Lord.»
2.46: Then the king gave orders to Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and he went out and struck down Shemei, who died. And the kingdom was established in Solomon’s hand.

3.1: Solomon allied himself by marriage with Pharaoh, king of Egypt. He took Pharaoh's daughter as his wife and brought her to the city of David, until he had finished building his house and the house of the Lord, as well as the wall surrounding Jerusalem.
3.2: Only the people sacrificed at the holy places, for no house had been built in the name of the Lord until those days.
3.3: Solomon loved the Lord, walking according to the precepts of David his father, only he offered sacrifices at the holy places and burned incense there.
3.4: The king went to Gibeon to offer sacrifices, for that was the great high place. Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings on that altar.
3.5: At Gibeon, the Lord appeared to Solomon in a dream during the night, and God said to him, «Ask for whatever you want me to give you.»
3.6: Solomon replied: «You have shown great kindness to your servant David, my father, as he walked before you in faithfulness, in righteousness, and in uprightness of heart toward you; you have maintained this great kindness to him and have given him a son who sits on his throne, as it appears today.
3.7: Now, Lord my God, you have made your servant king in place of David, my father, and I am but a very young man, not knowing how to conduct myself.
3.8: Your servant is in the midst of your people whom you have chosen, a vast people, which cannot be counted or measured, so numerous are they.
3.9: Therefore, give your servant an understanding heart to judge your people, to discern good from evil. For who is able to judge this great people of yours?»
3.10: The Lord was pleased that Solomon had made this request of him.,
3.11: And God said to him, «Because you have made this request, and have not asked for yourself for long life, nor for riches, nor for the death of your enemies, but have asked for yourself for understanding to exercise justice,
3.12: Behold, I do according to your word: behold, I give you a wise and understanding heart, so that there has been no one like you before you and there will be no one like you after you.
3.13: And even what you have not asked for, I give you, riches and honor, so that among kings there will be no one like you all your days.
3.14: And if you walk in my ways, keeping my statutes and my commandments, as David your father walked, I will prolong your days.»
3.15: Solomon awoke and realized it was a dream. Returning to Jerusalem, he stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord, offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and gave a feast for all his servants.
3.16: Then two women of ill repute came to the king and stood before him.
3.17: One of the women said, "Please, my lord. This woman and I lived in the same house, and I gave birth to a child near her in the house.
3.18: Three days after I gave birth to my child, this woman also gave birth to a child. We were together; no strangers were with us in the house, it was just the two of us in the house.
3.19: The woman's son died during the night because she had lain on top of him.
3.20: She got up in the middle of the night, took my son to my side while the servant slept, and laid him in her bosom, and her son who was dead, she laid him in my bosom.
3.21: When I got up in the morning to breastfeed my son, he was dead, but, having examined him carefully that morning, I realized that it was not my son I had given birth to.»
3.22: The other woman said, "No. It is my son who is alive and it is your son who is dead." But the first woman replied, "Not at all, it is your son who is dead and it is my son who is alive." And they argued before the king.
3.23: The king said, "One says, 'My son is alive, and your son is dead,' and the other says, 'No, your son is dead, and my son is alive.'"«
3.24: And the king said, "Bring me a sword." So they brought the sword before the king.
3.25: And the king said, "Divide the living child in two and give half to one and half to the other."«
3.26: Then the woman whose son was still alive said to the king, for her heart yearned for her son: «Oh, my lord, give him the living child and let him not be killed.» And the other said: «Let him be neither mine nor yours; share him.»
3.27: And the king replied and said, "Give the living child to the first woman, and do not kill him; she is his mother."«
3.28: All Israel heard of the judgment that the king had pronounced, and they feared the king, seeing that there was divine wisdom in him to administer justice.

4.1: King Solomon was king over all Israel.
4.2: These were the chiefs in his service: Azarias, son of Zadok, was the prime minister,
4.3: Elihoreph and Ahia, sons of Sisa, were secretaries; Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, was recorder;,
4.4: Banaiah, son of Jehoiada, was in command of the army, Zadok and Abiathar were priests,
4.5: Azarias, son of Nathan, was chief of the stewards; Zabud, son of Nathan, a priest, was a close advisor to the king.,
4.6: Ahisar was prefect of the palace and Adoniram, son of Abda, was in charge of forced labor.
4.7: Solomon had twelve overseers over all Israel; they provided for the maintenance of the king and his household, each one having to provide for one month of the year.
4.8: These are their names: Ben-Hur, in the mountains of Ephraim,
4.9: Ben-Decar, to Maccès, to Salebim, to Beth-Sames and to Elon of Bethanan,
4.10: Ben-Hesed, in Aruboth: he had Socho and all the region of Epher,
4.11: Ben-Abinadab, who had all the heights of Dor, and Tapheth, Solomon's daughter, was his wife,
4.12: Bana, son of Ahilud, who had Thanach and Mageddo and all Bethsan, which is near Sartan below Jezreel, from Bethsan to Abel-mehula, as far as beyond Jecmaan.
4.13: Ben-Gaber, in Ramoth-gilead: he had the towns of Jair, son of Manasseh, which are in Gilead, he had the region of Argob which is in Bashan, sixty large cities with walls and bronze bars,
4.14: Ahinadab, son of Addo, in Manaim,
4.15: Ahimaas, in Naphtali: he too had taken as his wife a daughter of Solomon, named Basemath,
4.16: Baanah, son of Husi, in Asher and Aloth,
4.17: Jehoshaphat, son of Pharaoh, in Issachar,
4.18: Shemei, son of Elah, in Benjamin,
4.19: Gabar, son of Uri, in the land of Gilead, the land of Sihon, king of the Amorites, and of Og, king of Bashan: there was only one overseer for that land.
4.20: Judah and Israel were as numerous as the sand on the seashore; they ate and drank and were merry.

5.1: Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms, from the river to the land of the Philistines and to the border of Egypt; they brought gifts and were subject to Solomon all the days of his life.
5.2: Solomon consumed daily the following provisions: thirty cors of fine flour and sixty cors of common flour,
5.3: ten fat oxen, twenty pasture oxen and one hundred sheep, not counting the deer, roe deer, fallow deer and fattened poultry.
5.4: For he ruled over all the land beyond the river, from Taphsah to Gaza, over all the kings beyond the river, and he had peace with all his subjects on all sides.
5.5: Judah and Israel dwelt securely, each under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan to Beersheba, all the days of Solomon.
5.6: Solomon had forty thousand stalls for the horses intended for his chariots and twelve thousand saddle horses.
5.7: The stewards provided for the upkeep of King Solomon and all those who were admitted to King Solomon's table, each during his month, they let nothing be lacking.
5.8: They also brought barley and straw for the draft and racing horses to the place where these were located, each according to what had been prescribed for him.
5.9: God gave Solomon wisdom, great understanding, and a mind as broad as the sand on the seashore.
5.10: Solomon's wisdom surpassed the wisdom of all the sons of the East and all the wisdom of Egypt.
5.11: He was wiser than any man, wiser than Ethan the Ezrahite, wiser than Heman, Chalcol and Dorda, the sons of Mahol, and his fame was widespread among all the surrounding nations.
5.12: He uttered three thousand maxims and his hymns numbered one thousand and five.
5.13: He discoursed on trees, from the cedar in Lebanon to the hyssop that grows out of the wall; he also discoursed on quadrupeds, birds, reptiles, and fish.
5.14: People came from all nations to hear the wisdom of Solomon, sent by all the kings of the earth who had heard of his wisdom.
5.15: Hiram, king of Tyre, sent his servants to Solomon, for he had learned that he had been anointed king in place of his father, and Hiram had always been a friend of David.
5.16: And Solomon sent word to Hiram:
5.17: «You know that David, my father, could not build a house in the name of the Lord his God, because of the wars with which his enemies surrounded him, until the Lord had put them under the sole of his feet.
5.18: Now, Lord my God has given me rest on all sides; there is no more adversary, no more troublesome affair.
5.19: And now I am thinking of building a house for the name of the Lord my God, as the Lord declared to David my father, saying: It is your son, whom I will put in your place on your throne, who will build the house for my name.
5.20: »Now, give orders to cut down cedars for me in Lebanon. My servants will be with your servants, and I will pay you whatever you ask for your servants’ wages, for you know that there is no one among us who knows how to cut wood like the Sidonians.”
5.21: When Hiram heard Solomon's words, he rejoiced greatly and said, "Blessed be the Lord today, who has given David a wise son to rule over this great people."«
5.22: And Hiram sent word to Solomon: «I have heard what you sent me to say, I will do what you desire concerning the cedar wood and the cypress wood.
5.23: My servants will bring them down from Lebanon to the sea, and I will float them by sea to the place you will tell me to. There I will untie them, and you will take them. And you will fulfill my desire by providing food for my household.»
5.24: Hiram gave Solomon as much cedar and cypress wood as he wanted,
5.25: Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand kors of wheat for the upkeep of his household and twenty kors of crushed olive oil. This is what Solomon delivered to Hiram every year.
5.26: And the Lord gave Solomon wisdom, as he had promised him, and there was peace between Hiram and Solomon, and they made a covenant together.
5.27: King Solomon conscripted forced laborers from among all the Israelites, and the forced laborers numbered thirty thousand.
5.28: He sent them to Lebanon, ten thousand a month alternately, they were one month in Lebanon and two months at home, Adoniram was in charge of the men on forced labor.
5.29: Solomon still had seventy thousand men to carry burdens and eighty thousand to quarry stones in the mountains,
5.30: not counting the superintendents appointed by Solomon for the works, numbering three thousand three hundred, they directed the people who worked on the projects.
5.31: The king ordered that large stones, choice stones, be extracted to establish the foundations of the house in hewn stone.
5.32: Solomon’s masons and Hiram’s masons and the Giblians cut and prepared the wood and stones to build the house.

6.1: In the four hundred and eightieth year after the children of Israel came out of the land of Egypt, in the fourth year of Solomon’s reign over Israel, in the month of Ziv, which is the second month, he built the house of the Lord.
6.2: The house that King Solomon built for the Lord was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high.
6.3: The portico in front of the temple of the house was twenty cubits long along the width of the house and ten cubits wide at the front of the house.
6.4: The king had fixed grilles installed in the house.
6.5: He built stories against the wall of the house, around the walls of the house, around the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place, and he made side chambers all around.
6.6: The lower floor was five cubits wide, the middle one six cubits wide, and the third seven cubits wide, because the walls of the house had been set back all around, outside, so that the beams would not enter the walls of the house.
6.7: When the house was built, it was built of stones all prepared in the quarry, and thus neither hammer, nor axe, nor any iron instrument was heard in the house while it was being built.
6.8: The entrance to the middle floor was on the right side of the house; one went up by winding staircases to the middle floor and from the middle floor to the third floor.
6.9: Solomon built the house and finished it; he covered the house with cedar beams and planks.
6.10: He built the stories attached to the whole house, making them five cubits high and linking them to the house with cedar beams.
6.11: And the word of the Lord came to Solomon, saying:
6.12: «This house that you are building… if you walk according to my laws, if you put my ordinances into practice, if you observe all my commandments, regulating your conduct according to them, I will fulfill to you my word that I spoke to David your father,
6.13: I will dwell among the children of Israel and I will not abandon my people Israel.»
6.14: And Solomon built the house and finished it.
6.15: He lined the interior walls of the house with cedar planks, from the floor of the house to the ceiling, he thus paneled the interior and he covered the floor of the house with cypress planks.
6.16: He covered the twenty cubits from the bottom of the house, from the floor to the top of the walls, with cedar planks, and he took from the house what was needed to make it a sanctuary, the Holy of Holies.
6.17: The house, that is, the earlier temple, was forty cubits long.
6.18: The cedar wood inside the house was carved into gourds and blooming flowers, everything was cedar, you couldn't see the stone.
6.19: Solomon placed the sanctuary inside the house, at the back, to place the ark of the covenant of the Lord there.
6.20: The inside of the sanctuary was twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and twenty cubits high. Solomon overlaid it with fine gold and he overlaid the altar with cedar.
6.21: Solomon overlaid the inside of the house with fine gold and closed the front of the sanctuary with gold chains, covering it with gold.
6.22: So he covered the whole house with gold, the entire house, and he covered the whole altar that was in front of the sanctuary with gold.
6.23: He made in the sanctuary two cherubim of wild olive wood, each ten cubits high.
6.24: One of the wings of each cherub was five cubits long, and the second wing of the cherub was five cubits long; there were ten cubits from the tip of one of its wings to the tip of the other.
6.25: The second cherub was also ten cubits long. The two cherubs were the same size and had the same shape.
6.26: The height of one cherub was ten cubits, and the same for the second cherub.
6.27: Solomon placed the cherubim in the middle of the inner house, with their wings spread out, the wing of the first touching one of the walls and the wing of the second cherub touching the other wall, and their other wings touching each other, wing to wing, towards the middle of the house.
6.28: And Solomon clothed the cherubim with gold.
6.29: He had cherubs, palm trees and blooming flowers sculpted in relief on all the walls of the house, all around, inside and out.
6.30: He covered the floor of the house with gold, both inside and out.
6.31: He made the doors of the sanctuary from wild olive wood; the frame with the posts took up one-fifth of the wall.
6.32: On the two leaves of wild olive wood, he had cherubs, palm trees and blooming flowers carved and he covered them with gold, spreading the gold on the cherubs and on the palm trees.
6.33: Similarly, he made posts of wild olive wood for the temple gate, which covered a quarter of the wall,
6.34: and two cypress wood leaves, the first leaf was formed of two folding sheets, the second leaf was similarly formed of two folding sheets.
6.35: He sculpted cherubs, palm trees and blooming flowers there and he covered them with gold, suitable for sculpture.
6.36: He built the inner courtyard with three rows of dressed stone and one row of cedar beams.
6.37: In the fourth year, in the month of Ziv, the foundations of the house of the Lord were laid,
6.38: And in the eleventh year, in the month of Bul, which is the eighth month, the house was completed in all its parts and as it should have been. Solomon built it in the space of seven years.

7.1: Solomon built his house in thirteen years and he completed it entirely.
7.2: He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon, which was one hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high; it rested on four rows of cedar columns, and there were cedar beams on the columns.
7.3: A cedar roof covered it, above the chambers which rested on the columns, forty-five in number, fifteen per row.
7.4: There were three rows of rooms and the windows faced each other, three times.
7.5: All the doors and all the posts were made of square planks and the windows faced each other, three times.
7.6: He made the columned portico, fifty cubits long and thirty cubits wide, and in front of it another portico with columns and steps in front of them.
7.7: He made the portico of the throne, where he administered justice, the portico of judgment, and he lined it with cedar from floor to ceiling.
7.8: His dwelling house was built in the same way, in a second courtyard, beyond the portico, and he made a house similar to this portico for Pharaoh's daughter, whom Solomon had married.
7.9: All these buildings were made of precious stones, cut according to measurements, sawn with a saw, inside and out, from the foundations to the cornices and outside to the great courtyard.
7.10: The foundations were also made of precious stones, large stones, stones measuring ten cubits and stones measuring eight cubits.
7.11: Above, there were still precious stones, cut according to measurements, and cedar wood.
7.12: The great courtyard had around its entire perimeter three rows of dressed stone and one row of cedar beams, like the inner courtyard of the Lord's house and like the portico of the house.
7.13: King Solomon sent for Hiram from Tyre.
7.14: He was the son of a widow from the tribe of Naphtali, but his father was a Tyrian and a bronze worker. He was full of wisdom, understanding, and knowledge to do all kinds of bronze work; he came to King Solomon and carried out all his projects.
7.15: He made the two bronze columns; the height of one column was eighteen cubits, and a line of twelve cubits measured the circumference of the second column.
7.16: He made two cast bronze capitals to place on the tops of the columns, the height of one capital was five cubits and the height of the second capital was five cubits.
7.17: There was a net-like decoration and a chain-like decoration on the capitals that surmounted the tops of the columns, seven on one capital, seven on the second capital.
7.18: He made two rows of pomegranates around one of the trellises, to cover the capital that surmounted one of the columns, and did the same for the second capital.
7.19: The capitals that were on top of the columns in the portico depicted lilies four cubits high.
7.20: The capitals placed on the two columns were surrounded by two hundred grenades; at the top, near the bulge that was beyond the trellis, there were also two hundred grenades arranged all around, on the second capital.
7.21: He erected the columns at the portico of the temple; he erected the right column and named it Jachin, then he erected the left column and named it Boaz.
7.22: And on the tops of the columns there was work depicting lilies. Thus the work on the columns was completed.
7.23: He made the sea of molten bronze. It was ten cubits from rim to rim, perfectly round, five cubits high, and a line thirty cubits measured its circumference.
7.24: Gourds surrounded it, below the rim, ten per cubit, going all around the sea in two rows, the gourds were melted with it in one piece.
7.25: It was placed on twelve oxen, three of which faced north, three faced west, three faced south, and three faced east; the sea was upon them, and the entire rear part of their bodies was hidden within.
7.26: Its thickness was one palm and its rim was like the rim of a cup, with a fleur-de-lis pattern. It contained two thousand baths.
7.27: He made the ten bronze bases, each one four cubits long, four cubits wide, and three cubits high.
7.28: This is how the bases were made: they were formed of panels, and the panels were fitted between frames.,
7.29: On the panels that were between the frames, there were lions, bulls and cherubs and on the frames, at the top, a support and below the lions, bulls and cherubs hung garlands.
7.30: Each base had four bronze wheels with bronze axles and its four feet had supports; these cast supports were below the basin and beyond the garlands.
7.31: The opening to receive the basin was inside the crowning of the base, it was one cubit high, this opening was round, in the shape of a column base and having one and a half cubits in diameter and on this opening there were also sculptures, the panels were square and not rounded.
7.32: The four wheels were below the panels and the wheel axles were fixed to the base, each wheel was one and a half cubits high.
7.33: The wheels were made like a chariot wheel; their axles, rims, spokes and hubs were all cast.
7.34: At the four corners of each base were four supports, and these supports were one piece with the base.
7.35: At the top of the base was a circle half a cubit high, and on the top of the base, its supports and panels were of a single piece.
7.36: On the support plates and on the panels, he engraved cherubs, lions and palm trees, according to the free space for each, and garlands all around.
7.37: That is how he made the ten bases, the same type of casting, the same dimensions, the same shape for all.
7.38: He made ten bronze basins, each basin held forty baths, each basin was four cubits in diameter, each basin rested on a base, one of ten bases.
7.39: He arranged the ten bases thus: five on the right side of the house and five on the left side of the house, and he placed the sea on the right side of the house, to the east, towards the south.
7.40: Hiram made the cauldrons, the shovels, and the bowls. Thus Hiram finished all the work he did for King Solomon in the house of the Lord:
7.41: the two columns, the two moldings of the capitals which are on the top of the columns, the two trellises to cover the two moldings of the capitals which are on the top of the columns,
7.42: the four hundred grenades for the two trellises, two rows of grenades per trellis, to cover the two ridges of the capitals which are on the columns,
7.43: the ten bases and the ten basins on the bases,
7.44: the sea and the twelve oxen under the sea,
7.45: The pots, shovels, and bowls. All these utensils that Hiram made for King Solomon in the house of the Lord were of polished bronze.
7.46: The king had them melted in the plain of the Jordan, in clay soil, between Sochoth and Sarthan.
7.47: Solomon left all these utensils unweighed, because they were in very large quantity, the weight of the bronze was not verified.
7.48: Solomon also made all the other utensils that were in the house of the Lord: the golden altar, the golden table on which the bread of the Presence was placed,
7.49: the pure gold candlesticks, five on the right and five on the left, in front of the oracle, with the flowers, the lamps, and the gold tongs,
7.50: the basins, knives, bowls, cups and censers of pure gold, as well as the gold hinges for the doors of the inner house, namely the Holy of Holies and for the doors of the house, namely the Holy.
7.51: Thus was completed all the work that King Solomon did in the house of the Lord, and Solomon brought what David his father had consecrated, the silver, the gold, and the vessels, and poured them into the treasuries of the house of the Lord.

8.1: Then King Solomon assembled near him in Jerusalem the elders of Israel and all the heads of the tribes, the princes of the families of the children of Israel, to bring up from the city of David, that is, from Zion, the ark of the covenant of the Lord.
8.2: All the men of Israel gathered around King Solomon in the month of Ethanim, which is the seventh month, during the festival.
8.3: When all the elders of Israel had arrived, the priests carried the ark.
8.4: They carried the ark of the Lord, as well as the tent of meeting and all the sacred utensils that were in the tent; it was the priests and the Levites who carried them.
8.5: King Solomon and the entire assembly of Israel, who had gathered around him, stood with him before the ark. They sacrificed sheep and oxen, which could not be counted or numbered because of their multitude.
8.6: The priests brought the ark of the covenant of the Lord to its place, in the sanctuary of the house, in the Holy of Holies, under the wings of the Cherubim.
8.7: For the cherubim spread their wings over the place of the ark, and the cherubim covered the ark and its poles from above.
8.8: The bars were so long that their ends could be seen from the holy place in front of the sanctuary, but they could not be seen from outside. They have been there to this day.
8.9: The only things in the ark were the two stone tablets that Moses had placed there on Mount Horeb when the Lord made a covenant with the children of Israel as they came out of the land of Egypt.
8.10: As the priests left the holy place, the cloud filled the house of the Lord.
8.11: The priests could not remain there to perform their service because of the cloud, for the glory of the Lord filled the house of the Lord.
8.12: Then Solomon said, «The Lord wants to dwell in darkness.
8.13: I have built a house that will be your home, a place for you to reside in forever.»
8.14: Then the king turned his face and blessed the whole assembly of Israel, and the whole assembly of Israel was standing.
8.15: And he said, «Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel, who spoke by his mouth to David my father and fulfilled by his hand what he had declared, saying:
8.16: From the day I brought my people Israel out of Egypt, I did not choose a city from among all the tribes of Israel to build a house in which to house my name, but I chose David to reign over my people Israel.
8.17: David, my father, intended to build a house in the name of the Lord, the God of Israel,
8.18: But the Lord said to David, my father: Since you had it in your heart to build a house for my name, you did well to have this heart.
8.19: However, you will not be the one to build the house, but your son, who will come from your body, will build the house for my name.
8.20: The Lord has fulfilled the word that he had spoken: I have risen in place of David my father and I have sat on the throne of Israel, as the Lord had said, and I have built the house in the name of the Lord, the God of Israel.
8.21: I have established a place there for the ark, in which is kept the covenant of the Lord, which he made with our ancestors when he brought them out of the land of Egypt.»
8.22: Solomon stood before the altar of the Lord, in the presence of all the assembly of Israel, and, stretching out his hands toward heaven,
8.23: He said, «Lord, God of Israel, there is no God like you, either in heaven above or on earth below: you who keep covenant and mercy toward your servants who walk wholeheartedly before you,
8.24: just as you have kept for your servant David, my father, what you said to him, what you declared with your mouth, you have accomplished with your hand, as it is seen this day.
8.25: Now, Lord, God of Israel, observe in favor of your servant David, my father, what you said to him in these words: You will never lack a descendant to sit on the throne of Israel before me, provided that your sons take heed to their way, walking before me as you have walked before me.
8.26: And now, God of Israel, let the word that you spoke to your servant David, my father, be fulfilled.
8.27: «But does God truly dwell on earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; how much less this house that I have built!”.
8.28: However, give heed, O Lord my God, to the prayer of your servant and to his supplication, listening to the joyful cry and the prayer which your servant utters before you today,
8.29: keeping your eyes open night and day toward this house, toward the place of which you have said, “My name shall be there,” and listening to the prayer that your servant prays toward this place.
8.30: Hear the supplication of your servant and of your people Israel, when they pray in this place; hear from your dwelling place, from heaven, hear and forgive.
8.31: «If anyone sins against his neighbor and is made to take an oath, if he comes and swears before your altar in this house,
8.32: Listen to him from heaven, act and judge your servants, condemning the guilty and bringing down his conduct upon his own head, declaring the innocent righteous and rewarding him according to his innocence.
8.33: «When your people Israel are defeated before the enemy because they have sinned against you, if they return to you and glorify your name, if they offer prayers and supplications to you in this house,
8.34: Listen to them from heaven, forgive the sin of your people Israel and bring them back to the land you gave to their fathers.
8.35: «When the heavens are shut up and there is no rain because they have sinned against you, if they pray toward this place and give glory to your name, and turn from their sins because you have grieved them,
8.36: Listen to them from heaven, forgive the sins of your servants and your people Israel, teaching them the right way in which they should walk, and send rain on the land you have given as an inheritance to your people.
8.37: «When famine strikes the land, when plague strikes, when blight strikes, when locusts and grasshoppers strike, when the enemy besieges your people in the land, in its gates, when any plague or disease strikes,
8.38: If one man, if all your people Israel, offer prayers and supplications, and each one, acknowledging the wound in his heart, stretches out his hands toward this house,
8.39: Listen to them from heaven, from your dwelling place, and forgive, act, and repay each one according to all their ways, you who know their heart, for you alone know the hearts of all the children of men.,
8.40: so that they may fear you all the days they live in the land you gave to our fathers.
8.41: «As for the foreigner, who is not of your people Israel, but who comes from a distant land because of your name,
8.42: for people will hear of your great name, your strong hand, and your outstretched arm, when they come to pray in this house,
8.43: Listen to him from heaven, from your place of residence, and do according to all that the foreigner asks of you, so that all the peoples of the earth may know your name, to fear you, as your people Israel do, and to know that your name is called upon this house that I have built.
8.44: «When your people go out to fight their enemy, following the path you have sent them on, and when they pray to the Lord, with their faces turned toward the city you have chosen and toward the house I have built for your name,
8.45: Hear from heaven their prayer and their supplication and grant them justice.
8.46: «When they sin against you—for there is no one who does not sin—and when you are angry with them and hand them over to the enemy, and their conqueror carries them off as captives to the land of the enemy, whether far away or near,
8.47: If they come to their senses in the land of their conquerors, if they repent and plead with you in the land of their tyrants, saying, “We have sinned, we have done wrong, we have committed crimes,”,
8.48: if they return to you with all their heart and soul, in the land of their enemies who took them captive, if they pray to you, turning their faces toward the land you gave to their ancestors, toward the city you chose, and toward the house I built for your name,
8.49: Listen from heaven, from your dwelling place, to their prayer and their supplication, and grant them justice.,
8.50: Forgive your people their transgressions and all the sins they have committed against you; make them a cause for compassion before their tyrants, so that they may have pity on them.,
8.51: for they are your people and your inheritance, whom you brought out of Egypt, out of the midst of an iron furnace:
8.52: so that your eyes may be open to the supplication of your servant and to the supplication of your people Israel, to hear them in all that they ask of you.
8.53: For you separated them from all the peoples of the earth to be your inheritance, as you declared through Moses your servant when you brought our ancestors out of Egypt, O Lord God.»
8.54: When Solomon had finished addressing all this prayer and supplication to the Lord, he rose from before the altar of the Lord, where he had been kneeling with his hands outstretched toward heaven.
8.55: Having risen, he blessed the entire assembly of Israel with a loud voice, saying:
8.56: «Blessed be the Lord who has given rest to his people Israel, according to all that he has said. Of all the good words that he spoke through Moses his servant, not one word has failed.”.
8.57: May the Lord our God be with us, as he was with our fathers; may he not abandon us or forsake us.,
8.58: but that he may incline our hearts to him, so that we may walk in all his ways and observe his commandments, his statutes, and his ordinances, which he prescribed to our fathers.
8.59: May my words of supplication that I have spoken before the Lord be near to the Lord our God, night and day, so that, according to the needs of each day, he may do justice to his servant and to his people Israel,
8.60: so that all the peoples of the earth may know that the Lord is God, that there is no other.
8.61: "Let your heart be wholly devoted to the Lord our God, to walk in his statutes and to keep his commandments, as we do today."»
8.62: The king and all Israel with him offered sacrifices before the Lord.
8.63: Solomon offered a peace offering to the Lord, twenty-two thousand oxen and one hundred and twenty thousand sheep. Thus the king and all the Israelites dedicated the house of the Lord.
8.64: On that day the king consecrated the middle of the courtyard which is in front of the house of the Lord, for there he offered the burnt offerings, the grain offerings and the fat of the peace offerings, because the bronze altar which is in front of the Lord was too small to hold the burnt offerings, the grain offerings and the fat of the peace offerings.
8.65: Solomon celebrated the festival at that time, and all Israel with him, a great multitude who came from the entrance of Emath to the Wadi of Egypt, before the Lord our God, for seven days and seven more days, that is, fourteen days.
8.66: On the eighth day he dismissed the people. And they blessed the king and went to their homes, joyful and glad of heart for all the good that the Lord had done to David his servant and to Israel his people.

9.1: When Solomon had finished building the house of the Lord and the king's house and everything that pleased Solomon, everything he desired to do,
9.2: The Lord appeared to him a second time, as he had appeared to him at Gibeon.
9.3: And the Lord said to him, «I have heard your prayer and your supplication which you uttered before me; I have consecrated this house which you have built, to put my name there forever, and there shall be my eyes and my heart forever.
9.4: And you, if you walk before me as David your father walked, with sincerity of heart and uprightness, putting into practice what I have commanded you, if you observe my statutes and my ordinances,
9.5: I will establish your royal throne in Israel forever, as I declared to David your father, saying: You will never lack a descendant to sit on the throne of Israel.
9.6: But if you and your children turn away from me, if you do not observe my commandments and my laws that I have set before you, and if you go and serve other gods and bow down to them,
9.7: I will exterminate Israel from the land I have given them, the house I have consecrated to my name; I will cast it out of my sight, and Israel will become a byword and an object of ridicule among all peoples.,
9.8: This house will always be high, but whoever passes by it will be astonished and will hiss. They will say: Why has the Lord done this to this land and this house?
9.9: And the answer will be: »Because they abandoned the Lord their God, who brought their ancestors out of Egypt, and instead followed other gods, bowing down to them and serving them, the Lord brought all these disasters upon them.”
9.10: After twenty years, when Solomon had built the two houses, the house of the Lord and the house of the king,
9.11: Hiram, king of Tyre, had supplied Solomon with cedar wood and cypress wood and gold, as much as he wanted; King Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities in the land of Galilee.
9.12: Hiram left Tyre to see the cities that Solomon was giving him, but they did not please him.,
9.13: And he said, "What are these cities that you have given me, my brother?" And he called them the land of Chabul, their name to this day.
9.14: Hiram had sent Solomon one hundred and twenty talents of gold.
9.15: This is what concerns the forced laborers that King Solomon raised up to build the house of the Lord and his own house, Mello and the wall of Jerusalem, Hesher, Mageddo and Gazer.
9.16: Pharaoh, king of Egypt, had gone up and taken Gazer. After burning it down and killing the Canaanites who lived in the city, he gave it as a dowry to his daughter, Solomon's wife.
9.18: Baalath and Thadmor in the desert land,
9.19: all the store cities that belonged to Solomon, the chariot cities, the cavalry cities, and everything that Solomon liked to build in Jerusalem, in Lebanon, and throughout the land under his dominion.
9.20: All the people who remained of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, who were not of the children of Israel,
9.21: namely, their descendants who had remained after them in the land and whom the children of Israel had not been able to devote to anathema, Solomon raised them up as forced laborers, which they have been to this day.
9.22: But Solomon did not take any of the children of Israel into slavery, for they were men of war, his servants, his leaders, his officers, the commanders of his chariots and his cavalry.
9.23: The chief inspectors of Solomon’s works were five hundred and fifty in number, charged with commanding the people engaged in the works.
9.24: Pharaoh's daughter went up from the City of David to her house which Solomon had built for her; it was then that he built Mello.
9.25: Three times a year Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar he had built to the Lord, and he burned incense on the altar before the Lord. Thus he finished building the temple.
9.26: King Solomon built a fleet at Asiongaber, which is near Ailath, on the shores of the Red Sea, in the land of Edom.
9.27: And Hiram sent his own servants, sailors who knew the sea, onto the ships to Solomon’s servants.
9.28: They went to Ophir and took from there four hundred and twenty talents of gold, which they brought to King Solomon.

10.1: The Queen of Sheba, having heard of Solomon's fame, came in the name of the Lord to test him with riddles.
10.2: She came to Jerusalem with a very large entourage, camels carrying spices, a great quantity of gold, and precious stones. She went to Solomon and told him everything that was in her heart.
10.3: Solomon answered all his questions: there was nothing that remained hidden from the king, without him being able to answer it.
10.4: When the Queen of Sheba saw all the wisdom of Solomon and the house he had built,
10.5: and the food on his table, and the apartments of his servants, and the rooms and clothing of his attendants, his cupbearers, and the stairway by which he went up into the house of the Lord—she was beside herself,
10.6: And she said to the king, "So it was true what I heard in my country about you and your wisdom.
10.7: I didn't believe the story until I came and saw it with my own eyes, and yet I hadn't been told half of it. You surpass in wisdom and magnificence what fame had made known to me.
10.8: Blessed are your people, blessed are your servants, who continually stand before you, who hear your wisdom.
10.9: Blessed be the Lord your God, who has been pleased with you and has placed you on the throne of Israel. Because the Lord loves Israel forever, he has made you king to do justice and righteousness.»
10.10: She gave the king one hundred and twenty talents of gold, a great quantity of spices, and precious stones. Never again did so many spices come as the Queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.
10.11: Hiram's ships, which brought gold from Ophir, also brought from Ophir a very large quantity of sandalwood and precious stones.
10.12: The king made balustrades from the sandalwood for the Lord's house and for the king's house, and harps and lyres for the singers. No more of this sandalwood was produced, and none has been seen since.
10.13: King Solomon gave the Queen of Sheba everything she desired and asked for, not to mention the gifts, in keeping with the power of a king like Solomon. Then she returned to her own country, she and her servants.
10.14: The weight of gold that reached Solomon in one year was six hundred and sixty-six talents of gold,
10.15: in addition to what he received from street vendors and the trade of merchants, from all the kings of Arabia and the governors of the country.
10.16: King Solomon made two hundred large shields of hammered gold, using six hundred shekels of gold for each shield,
10.17: and three hundred small shields of beaten gold, using three mines of gold for each shield, and the king put them in the house in the forest of Lebanon.
10.18: The king made a great throne of ivory and covered it with pure gold.
10.19: This throne had six steps and the upper part of the throne was rounded at the back; there were arms on each side of the seat, and two lions stood near the arms.,
10.20: And twelve lions stood there, on the six steps, six on each side. Nothing like it has ever been done in any other kingdom.
10.21: All of King Solomon's glasses were made of gold, and all the dishes in the House of the Forest of Lebanon were of pure gold. Nothing was made of silver; it was not valued at all in Solomon's time.
10.22: For the king had ships of Tharsis at sea with the ships of Hiram; once every three years, the ships of Tharsis would arrive, bringing gold and silver, ivory, monkeys and peacocks.
10.23: King Solomon was greater than all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.
10.24: Everyone was looking for Solomon, to hear the wisdom that God had placed in his heart.
10.25: And each one brought their gift, silver and gold objects, clothing, weapons, spices, horses and mules, every year.
10.26: Solomon assembled chariots and cavalry; he had fourteen hundred chariots and twelve thousand horsemen, whom he placed in the cities where his chariots were stored and near the king in Jerusalem.
10.27: The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones, and he made cedars as numerous as sycamores growing in the plain.
10.28: Solomon's horses came from Egypt; a caravan of the king's merchants would take them in droves at an agreed price:
10.29: A chariot was brought up and out of Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver and a horse for one hundred and fifty shekels. They also brought them out in the same way, by themselves, for all the kings of the Hittites and for the kings of Syria.

11.1: King Solomon loved many foreign women besides Pharaoh's daughter: Moabite, Ammonite, Edomite, Sidonian, and Hittite women.,
11.2: of the nations of which the Lord had said to the children of Israel: «You shall have no dealings with them and they shall have no dealings with you, otherwise they will turn your hearts after their gods.» Solomon cleaved to these nations out of love.
11.3: He had seven hundred princess wives and three hundred concubines, and his wives turned his heart away.
11.4: In Solomon’s old age, his wives turned his heart to other gods, and his heart was not wholly devoted to the Lord his God, as the heart of David his father had been.
11.5: Solomon went after Astarte, goddess of the Sidonians, and after Melchom, the abomination of the Ammonites.
11.6: And Solomon did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, and did not follow the Lord fully, as David his father had done.
11.7: Then Solomon built, on the mountain opposite Jerusalem, a high place for Hamosh, the abomination of Moab, and for Molech, the abomination of the Ammonites.
11.8: He did the same to all his foreign wives, who burned incense and offered sacrifices to their gods.
11.9: The Lord was angry with Solomon because he had turned his heart away from the Lord, the God of Israel, who had appeared to him twice,
11.10: and had forbidden him to go after other gods on this matter, but Solomon did not observe what the Lord had commanded.
11.11: And the Lord said to Solomon, «Because you have acted in this way and have not kept my covenant and my statutes which I commanded you, I will surely tear the kingdom from your hand and give it to your servant.
11.12: But I will not do it during your lifetime, because of David your father; I will tear it out from the hand of your son.
11.13: »And yet I will not tear away the whole kingdom; I will leave one tribe to your son, for the sake of David my servant and for the sake of Jerusalem, which I have chosen.”
11.14: The Lord raised up an enemy against Solomon: Adad the Edomite, of the royal line of Edom.
11.15: In the time when David was at war with Edom, when Joab, the commander of the army, went up to bury the dead, killed all the males who were in Edom,
11.16: Joab remained there for six months with all Israel, until he had exterminated all the males in Edom,
11.17: Adad fled with some Edomites, from among his father's servants, to go to Egypt; Adad was still a young boy.
11.18: Having left Midian, they went to Pharan, took with them some men from Pharan, and arrived in Egypt at Pharaoh, king of Egypt, who gave Adad a house, provided for his sustenance, and granted him land.
11.19: Adad found favor in the eyes of Pharaoh, to the point of giving him as a wife the sister of his wife, the sister of Queen Taphnes.
11.20: Taphnes' sister bore him a son, Genubath, whom Taphnes weaned in Pharaoh's house, and Genubath lived in Pharaoh's house among Pharaoh's children.
11.21: When Adad learned in Egypt that David had died and that Joab, the commander of the army, was dead, he said to Pharaoh, "Let me go to my own country."«
11.22: And Pharaoh said to him, "What do you lack with me, that you wish to go to your own country?" He replied, "Nothing, but let me go."«
11.23: God raised up another enemy against Solomon: Rezon, son of Eliada, who had fled from his master Hadarezer, king of Zobah.
11.24: He had gathered people around him and was the leader of a band, when David massacred his master's troops. They went to Damascus and settled there and reigned in Damascus.
11.25: He was an enemy of Israel throughout Solomon's lifetime, in addition to the harm inflicted upon him by Adad, and he harbored a deep aversion to Israel. He ruled over Syria.
11.26: Jeroboam also raised his hand against the king; he was the son of Nebat, an Ephrathite from Zareda, his mother was a widow named Sarva, and he was a servant of Solomon.
11.27: This was the cause of his rebellion against the king. Solomon was building Mello and closing the breach in the city of David, his father.
11.28: This Jeroboam was strong and valiant, and Solomon, having seen how active this young man was in his work, appointed him overseer of all the forced laborers in the house of Joseph.
11.29: At that time, Jeroboam, having left Jerusalem, was met on the road by the prophet Ahijah of Shiloh, wearing a new cloak; they were both alone in the fields.
11.30: Ahias, seizing the new cloak he was wearing, tore it into twelve pieces,
11.31: And he said to Jeroboam, «Take ten pieces for yourself. For thus says the Lord, the God of Israel: Behold, I am going to tear the kingdom from the hand of Solomon and I will give you ten tribes.
11.32: Only one tribe will remain for him, for the sake of my servant David and for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel:
11.33: And this is because they have forsaken me and bowed down to Astarte, goddess of the Sidonians, to Hamos, god of Moab, and to Melchom, god of the Ammonites, and because they have not walked in my ways to do what is right in my eyes and to observe my statutes and my ordinances, as David, Solomon's father, did.
11.34: Nevertheless, I will not take any part of the kingdom from his hand, but I will maintain him as prince all the days of his life, for the sake of David my servant, whom I have chosen and who has kept my commandments and my laws.
11.35: It is from the hand of his son that I will take the kingdom away and I will give you ten tribes.
11.36: I will give a tribe to his son, so that David, my servant, may always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem, the city that I have chosen to put my name there.
11.37: I will take you and you will reign over all that your soul desires, and you will be king over Israel.
11.38: If you obey all that I command you, if you walk in my ways and do what is right in my eyes, observing my statutes and my commandments, as David my servant did, I will be with you, I will build you a lasting house, as I built one for David, and I will give you Israel.
11.39: For I will humble David's descendants because of his unfaithfulness, but not forever.»
11.40: Solomon sought to kill Jeroboam, but Jeroboam arose and fled to Egypt, to Shesak, king of Egypt; he was in Egypt until the death of Solomon.
11.41: The rest of Solomon's actions, all that he did and his wisdom, are they not written in the book of Solomon's acts?
11.42: The time that Solomon reigned in Jerusalem, over all Israel, was forty years.
11.43: And Solomon rested with his ancestors and was buried in the city of David his father. Rehoboam his son reigned in his place.

12.1: Rehoboam went to Shechem, for all Israel had come to Shechem to make him king.
12.2: Jeroboam, son of Nebat, having learned what was happening, was still in Egypt where he had fled from King Solomon, and Jeroboam remained in Egypt.,
12.3: They sent for him. Then Jeroboam and all the assembly of Israel came and spoke to Rehoboam, saying:
12.4: «"Your father made our yoke harsh; now you must lighten the harsh servitude your father imposed on us and the heavy yoke he put upon us, and we will serve you."»
12.5: He told them, "Go away for three days and come back to me." And the people went away.
12.6: King Rehoboam consulted the elders who had stood with Solomon, his father, during his lifetime, saying, «What do you advise me to say to these people?»
12.7: They spoke to him, saying, «If today you are helpful to these people, if you assist them, if you answer them and speak kindly to them, they will be your servants forever.»
12.8: But Rehoboam disregarded the advice given to him by the elders and consulted the young men who had grown up with him and who stood before him.
12.9: He said to them, "What do you advise me to say to these people who speak to me, 'Lighten the yoke your father imposed on us'?"«
12.10: The young men who had grown up with him replied, saying: «This is what you will say to the people who spoke to you: ‘Your father made our yoke heavy, you lighten it for us.’ This is what you will say to them: ‘My little finger is thicker than my father’s loins.’”.
12.11: "Well then. My father burdened you with a heavy yoke, and I will make your yoke even heavier; my father chastised you with whips, and I will chastise you with scorpions."»
12.12: Jeroboam and all the people came to Rehoboam on the third day, as the king had said, «Return to me in three days.»
12.13: The king responded harshly to the people. Ignoring the advice the elders had given him,
12.14: He spoke to them according to the advice of the young men, saying: «My father made your yoke heavy, and I will make your yoke even heavier; my father chastised you with whips, and I will chastise you with scorpions.»
12.15: The king did not listen to the people, for this was the Lord's way of fulfilling the word that the Lord had spoken through Ahijah the Shilonite to Jeroboam, son of Nebat.
12.16: When all Israel saw that the king would not listen to them, the people answered the king, saying, «What share do we have in David? We have no inheritance in the son of Jesse. Go to your tents, Israel. As for you, David, see to your own house.» So Israel went to their tents.
12.17: It was only over the children of Israel who lived in the cities of Judah that Rehoboam reigned.
12.18: Then King Rehoboam sent Aduram, who was in charge of taxes, but Aduram was stoned to death by all Israel. And King Rehoboam quickly mounted a chariot to flee to Jerusalem.
12.19: This is how Israel separated itself from the house of David to this day.
12.20: When all Israel heard that Jeroboam had returned from Egypt, they sent and summoned him to the assembly and made him king over all Israel. No one followed the house of David except the tribe of Judah.
12.21: Back in Jerusalem, Rehoboam gathered all the house of Judah and the tribe of Benjamin, one hundred and eighty thousand elite warriors, to fight against the house of Israel, in order to bring the kingdom back to Rehoboam, son of Solomon.
12.22: But the word of God came to Shemaiah, the man of God, saying:
12.23: «Speak to Rehoboam, son of Solomon, king of Judah, and to all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and to the remnant of the people, saying:
12.24: Thus says the Lord: Do not go up and fight against your brothers, the children of Israel. Return each to your home, for this thing has happened because of me.» They listened to the word of the Lord and returned, in accordance with the word of the Lord.
12.25: Jeroboam built Shechem on the mountain of Ephraim and he remained there, then he went out and built Phanuel.
12.26: And Jeroboam said in his heart, «Now the kingdom may well return to the house of David.
12.27: If this people goes up to offer sacrifices in the house of the Lord in Jerusalem, their hearts will return to their lord, to Rehoboam king of Judah; they will kill me and return to Rehoboam king of Judah.»
12.28: After consulting with one another, the king made two golden calves and said to the people, «You have gone up to Jerusalem long enough. Here are your God, Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt.»
12.29: He placed one of these calves at Bethel and the other at Dan.
12.30: This was an occasion of sin, for the people went as far as Dan to worship one of the calves.
12.31: Jeroboam made a house of sacred places and appointed priests from among the people, who were not children of Levi.
12.32: Jeroboam instituted a festival in the eighth month, on the fifteenth day of the month, following the example of the festival celebrated in Judah, and he offered sacrifices on the altar. He did this at Bethel, so that sacrifices might be offered to the calves he had made. He also appointed priests of the high places he had erected at Bethel.
12.33: He went up onto the altar he had made at Bethel on the fifteenth day of the eighth month, the month he had chosen of his own accord. He instituted a festival for the children of Israel and went up onto the altar to burn the sacrifices.

13.1: Behold, a man of God came, in the word of the Lord, from Judah to Bethel, while Jeroboam stood at the altar to set fire to the sacrifices.
13.2: He cried out against the altar, in the word of the Lord, and said: «Altar! Altar! This is what the Lord says: A son will be born to the house of David, his name will be Josiah. He will sacrifice on you the priests of the high places who offer fire sacrifices on you, and human bones will be burned on you.»
13.3: And that same day he gave a sign, saying, «This is the sign that the Lord has spoken: behold, the altar will split apart and the ashes that are on it will be poured out.»
13.4: When King Jeroboam heard the word that the man of God had cried out against the altar at Bethel, he reached out his hand from the altar, saying, «Seize him.» But the hand that he stretched out against him withered, and he could not draw it back to himself.
13.5: The altar split open and the ashes fell from the altar, according to the sign given by the man of God in the word of the Lord.
13.6: The king spoke and said to the man of God, «Appease the Lord your God and pray for me, so that my hand may be restored to me.» The man of God appeased the Lord, and the king was able to regain his hand, which became as it had been before.
13.7: The king said to the man of God, "Come with me into the house and be refreshed, and I will give you a present."«
13.8: The man of God answered the king, «Even if you were to give me half your house, I would not go in with you, nor would I eat bread or drink water in that place.”,
13.9: For this command was given me in the word of the Lord: »You shall not eat bread, nor drink water, nor return by the way you came.”
13.10: So he went away by another way and did not return by the way by which he had come to Bethel.
13.11: Now there was an old prophet who lived in Bethel, and his sons came and told him all the things that the man of God had done that day in Bethel, and they also reported to their father the words that he had spoken to the king.
13.12: And their father said to them, «Which way did he go?» For his sons had seen which way the man of God who had come from Judah had gone.
13.13: And he said to his sons, "Saddle the donkey for me." So they saddled the donkey for him, and he mounted it.
13.14: He went after the man of God and, finding him sitting under the oak tree, said to him, «Are you the man of God who came from Judah?» He answered, «I am.»
13.15: The prophet said to him, "Come home with me and you will eat some bread."«
13.16: But he replied, «I cannot go back with you, nor enter with you; I will not eat bread or drink water with you in this place.”,
13.17: For it was said to me in the word of the Lord: »You shall not eat bread there, nor drink water there, nor return by the way you came.”
13.18: And he said to him, «I too am a prophet like you, and an angel spoke to me in the word of the Lord, saying: Bring him back with you to your house, so that he may eat bread and drink water.» He was lying to him.
13.19: The man of God returned with him and ate bread and drank water in his house.
13.20: As they sat at the table, the word of the Lord came to the prophet who had brought him back,
13.21: And he cried out to the man who had come from Judah, saying, «This is what the Lord says: Because you have rebelled against the Lord’s command and have not kept the command the Lord your God gave you,
13.22: because you returned and ate bread and drank water in the place of which the Lord had told you: »You shall not eat bread or drink water there, and your body shall not enter the tomb of your ancestors.”
13.23: After he had eaten bread and drunk, the old prophet saddled the donkey for him, that is to say, for the prophet he had brought back.
13.24: The man of God having gone away, he was met on the road by a lion, which killed him. While his body lay on the road, the donkey remained beside him and the lion remained beside the body.
13.25: And behold, some people who were passing by saw the corpse lying on the road and the lion standing beside the corpse, and they spoke of it when they arrived in the city where the old prophet lived.
13.26: When the prophet who had brought the man of God back from the way heard about it, he said, «This is the man of God who rebelled against the Lord’s command, and the Lord handed him over to the lion, which tore him to pieces and killed him, according to the word the Lord had spoken to him.»
13.27: Then he said to his sons, "Saddle the donkey for me." When they had saddled it,
13.28: He went and found the corpse lying on the road, and the donkey and the lion standing beside it. The lion had not devoured the corpse, nor had it torn the donkey to pieces.
13.29: The prophet picked up the body of the man of God and, having placed it on the donkey, he brought it back and the old prophet returned to the city to mourn him and to bury him.
13.30: He placed the body in its tomb, and they wept over him, saying, "Alas, my brother."«
13.31: When he had buried him, he said to his sons, «When I die, you shall bury me in the tomb where the man of God is buried, and you shall lay my bones beside his bones.
13.32: For the word that he cried out in the word of the Lord against the altar that is in Bethel and against all the houses of the holy places that are in the cities of Samaria will be fulfilled.»
13.33: After this event, Jeroboam did not turn away from his evil way; again he created priests of the holy places from among the people; whoever desired it, he consecrated, and that person became a priest of the holy places.
13.34: In this they sinned against the house of Jeroboam, and that is why it was destroyed and exterminated from the face of the earth.

14.1: At that time, Abijah, son of Jeroboam, became ill.
14.2: Jeroboam said to his wife, «Get up, I beg you, and disguise yourself so that no one will know you are Jeroboam’s wife, and go to Shiloh. Behold, Ahijah the prophet is there, the one who told me that I would be king over this people.”.
14.3: Take with you ten loaves of bread, some cakes, and a jar of honey, and go to his house: he will tell you what is to happen to the child.»
14.4: Jeroboam's wife did this; she got up, went to Shiloh, and entered the house of Ahijah. Now Ahijah could no longer see, because old age had dimmed his eyes.
14.5: The Lord had said to Ahijah: «Here is Jeroboam’s wife coming to ask you for some word about her son, who is sick; you shall speak to her in such and such a way. When she arrives, she will pretend to be someone else.”.
14.6: When Ahijah heard the sound of her footsteps as she crossed the threshold, he said, «Come in, wife of Jeroboam, why are you pretending to be someone else? I have a difficult message for you.
14.7: Go, tell Jeroboam: This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: I raised you up from among the people and appointed you ruler over my people Israel,
14.8: I tore the kingdom from the house of David and gave it to you, but you were not like my servant David, who kept my commandments and walked after me with all his heart, doing only what was right in my eyes,
14.9: But you have done more evil than all who came before you; you have gone and made other gods and cast images to provoke me, and you have cast me behind your back.
14.10: Therefore, behold, I am going to bring disaster upon the house of Jeroboam, I will exterminate every male belonging to Jeroboam, both slave and free, and Israel, and I will sweep away the house of Jeroboam, as one sweeps away dung, until none of it remains.
14.11: Anyone from the house of Jeroboam who dies in the city will be eaten by dogs, and anyone who dies in the fields will be eaten by birds of the air: for the Lord has spoken.
14.12: And you, get up, go to your house; as soon as your feet enter the city, the child will die.
14.13: All Israel will mourn for him and bury him, for he is the only one of the house of Jeroboam who will be put in a tomb, because he is the only one of the house of Jeroboam in whom anything good was found in the sight of the Lord, the God of Israel.
14.14: The Lord will establish a king over Israel who will exterminate the house of Jeroboam on that day. But what? It's already happening.
14.15: The Lord will strike Israel, as the reed is shaken in the midst of the waters; he will uproot Israel from this good land which he gave to their fathers and scatter them beyond the river, because they have made themselves Asherahs, provoking the Lord to anger.
14.16: He will give Israel up because of the sins of Jeroboam, which he committed and caused Israel to commit.»
14.17: Jeroboam's wife got up and set out, and arrived at Terah. As she crossed the threshold of the house, the child died.
14.18: They will bury him and all Israel will mourn for him, according to the word that the Lord had spoken through his servant Ahijah the prophet.
14.19: The rest of the acts of Jeroboam, how he waged war and how he reigned, are written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
14.20: Jeroboam reigned for twenty-two years, and he rested with his ancestors. Nadab, his son, succeeded him as king.
14.21: Rehoboam, son of Solomon, reigned in Judah. He was forty-one years old when he became king, and he reigned seventeen years in Jerusalem, the city the Lord had chosen out of all the tribes of Israel to put his name there. His mother's name was Naamah the Ammonite.
14.22: Judah did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, and by the sins they committed they aroused his jealousy more than their fathers had done.
14.23: They too built sacred places for themselves with stelae and Asherahs, on every high hill and under every green tree.
14.24: There were even prostitutes in the land. They acted according to all the abominations of the nations that the Lord had driven out before the children of Israel.
14.25: In the fifth year of Rehoboam's reign, Shesack, king of Egypt, came up against Jerusalem.
14.26: He took the treasures of the house of the Lord and the treasures of the king's house: he took everything. He took all the golden shields that Solomon had made.
14.27: In their place, King Rehoboam made bronze shields and gave them to the chiefs of the guards who guarded the entrance to the king's house.
14.28: Each time the king went to the Lord's house, the guards carried them and then brought them back to the guardroom.
14.29: The rest of the acts of Rehoboam and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
14.30: There was always war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam.
14.31: Rehoboam rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David. His mother was named Naamah the Ammonite, and Abiam her son reigned in her place.

15.1: In the eighteenth year of King Jeroboam son of Nebat, Abiam became king of Judah,
15.2: and he reigned three years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Maacha, daughter of Abessalom.
15.3: He walked in all the sins of his father that he had committed before him, and his heart was not wholly devoted to the Lord, as the heart of David, his father, had been.
15.4: But for David’s sake the Lord his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem, appointing his son after him and preserving Jerusalem.
15.5: For David had done what was right in the eyes of the Lord, and he had not turned aside all his life from any of the commandments he had received from him, except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite.
15.6: There was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam as long as he lived.
15.7: The rest of the acts of Abiam and all that he did are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah? There was war between Abiam and Jeroboam.
15.8: Abiam rested with his ancestors and was buried in the city of David. Asa, his son, reigned in his place.
15.9: In the twentieth year of Jeroboam, king of Israel, Asa became king of Judah,
15.10: and he reigned forty-one years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Maacha, daughter of Abessalom.
15.11: Asa did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, just as his father David had done.
15.12: He removed the prostitutes from the land and took away all the idols that his ancestors had made.
15.13: He even removed the title of queen mother from Maacha, his mother, because she had made an abominable idol for Astarte. Asa cut down her abominable idol and burned it in the Kidron Valley.
15.14: But the sacred places did not disappear, although Asa's heart was perfectly devoted to the Lord throughout his life.
15.15: He put into the house of the Lord the things consecrated by his father and the things consecrated by himself, silver, gold, and vessels.
15.16: There was war between Asa and Baasha, king of Israel, all their lives.
15.17: Baasha, king of Israel, came up against Judah and built Ramah, to prevent the people of Asa, king of Judah, from going out and coming in.
15.18: Asa took all the silver and gold that remained in the treasuries of the house of the Lord and the treasuries of the king's house and put them in the hands of his servants, and King Asa sent these servants to Ben-Hadad, son of Tabremon, son of Hezion, king of Syria, who lived in Damascus, to say:
15.19: «Let there be a covenant between me and you, as there was between my father and your father. I am sending you a gift of silver and gold. Go, break your covenant with Baasha, king of Israel, so that he will depart from me.»
15.20: Ben-Hadad listened to King Asa, he sent the commanders of his army against the cities of Israel and he defeated Ahion, Dan, Abel-Beth-Maahah and all of Ceneroth with all the land of Naphtali.
15.21: Baasa, having learned of this, ceased building Rama and remained in Tersha.
15.22: King Asa summoned all Judah, without exempting anyone, and they took away the stones and timber with which Baasha was building Ramah, and King Asa built with them Gibeah of Benjamin and Masphah.
15.23: The rest of all the acts of Asa, all his exploits and all that he did and the cities that he built, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Judah? However, in the days of his old age, he had a foot ailment.
15.24: Asa rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David, his father, and Jehoshaphat his son reigned in his place.
15.25: Nadab, son of Jeroboam, became king of Israel in the second year of Asa, king of Judah, and he reigned two years over Israel.
15.26: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, and he walked in the way of his father and in the sins of his father which he had caused Israel to commit.
15.27: Baasha, son of Ahijah, of the house of Issachar, conspired against him, and Baasha struck him down at Gebbethon, which belonged to the Philistines, for Nadab and all Israel were besieging Gebbethon.
15.28: Baasha put him to death in the third year of Asa, king of Judah, and he reigned in his place.
15.29: When he became king, he struck down the entire house of Jeroboam, sparing not a single living soul from the house of Jeroboam, without exterminating it, according to the word that the Lord had spoken through his servant Ahijah of Shiloh,
15.30: because of the sins of Jeroboam which he had committed and which he had caused Israel to commit, thus provoking the Lord, the God of Israel.
15.31: The rest of the acts of Nadab and all that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
15.32: There was war between Asa and Baasha, king of Israel, all their lives.
15.33: In the third year of Asa king of Judah, Baasha son of Ahijah became king over all Israel at Terah, and he reigned twenty-four years.
15.34: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord and walked in the way of Jeroboam and in the sins which Jeroboam had caused Israel to commit.

16.1: The word of the Lord came to Jehu, son of Hanani, against Baasha, saying:
16.2: «I raised you from the dust and appointed you leader of my people Israel, but you walked in the way of Jeroboam and caused my people Israel to sin, provoking me to anger with their sins.
16.3: Therefore, behold, I will sweep away Baasha and his house, and I will make your house like the house of Jeroboam, son of Nebat.
16.4: Anyone from the house of Baasha who dies in the city will be eaten by dogs, and anyone from his household who dies in the fields will be eaten by birds of the air.»
16.5: The rest of Baasha's deeds, what he did and his exploits, are they not written in the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
